required.title: [Read]
required.author: Jay Heinrichs (Young Readers' Edition or full edition)

competence_markers.description: The student can define and explain ethos, pathos, and logos with specific examples from analyzed speeches
The student can construct a complete argument using the Toulmin model — claim, evidence, warrant, qualifier, and rebuttal
The student can identify at least five rhetorical devices in published speeches and explain the effect each creates
The student can analyze a published speech using the analysis protocol: thesis, structure, appeals, devices, evidence, effectiveness
The student can identify at least four logical fallacies and has demonstrated the ability to catch them in their own reasoning
The student can articulate the difference between persuasion and manipulation with specific examples and the "transparency test"
The student's own arguments (written and spoken) show deliberate use of ethos, pathos, and logos

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain ethos, pathos, and logos in your own words.
narration_prompts.detail: Give an example of each from a speech you have analyzed.

narration_prompts.prompt: Construct a complete argument using the Toulmin model.
narration_prompts.detail: Include claim, evidence, warrant, qualifier, and rebuttal. On any topic you care about.
What is the thesis? What appeals does the speaker use? What devices? Is the evidence strong? Is the speech effective?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between honest emotional appeal (pathos) and emotional manipulation?
Name three logical fallacies and explain each one.
narration_prompts.detail: Then: have you ever caught yourself committing one? What happened?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a rhetorical device and explain how it works.
narration_prompts.detail: Find three examples of it in published speeches. Why does it make language more powerful?

cross_connections.description: The defensive counterpart. 1.7 teaches you to detect fallacies and manipulation aimed at you. 3.3.9-B1 teaches you to avoid them in your own arguments. Together they produce a person who can both defend against and deploy rhetorical skill with integrity.
Public speaking is the delivery vehicle for rhetorical argument. 3.3.6 builds how you deliver; 3.3.9-B1 builds what you deliver and why it is logically compelling.

Written argument and spoken argument use the same Toulmin structure, the same appeals, and the same devices. The essay and the speech are different delivery vehicles for the same rhetorical architecture.
Rhetorical power depends on linguistic precision. Vocabulary, syntax, and understanding of how language creates meaning are the raw materials of rhetoric.
The ethical framework for rhetoric — when is persuasion legitimate, when does it become manipulation, what obligations does a speaker have to truth — is a philosophical question.
Ethos is built on character. The speaker whose integrity is genuine has an ethos that no technique can replicate. The speaker whose character is compromised has an ethos that no technique can save.

content.why_this_matters:
Over two thousand years ago, a Greek philosopher named Aristotle figured out something that has been true ever since: there are exactly three ways to persuade another human being. You can persuade them with your character and credibility (**ethos**). You can persuade them with emotion (**pathos**). Or you can persuade them with logic and evidence (**logos**). Every effective argument in the history of the world — every great speech, every winning court case, every movement that changed society — uses some combination of these three.

This is not ancient trivia. This is the operating system of persuasion. When you understand ethos, pathos, and logos, you understand why some arguments work and others fail. You understand why a speaker with bad evidence but great charisma can still win a crowd. You understand why a speaker with perfect logic but no emotional connection puts the audience to sleep. You understand how to construct arguments that are effective AND honest — because the same tools can be used to serve truth or to destroy it.

This packet gives you the classical toolkit. You will learn Aristotle's three appeals. You will learn the structure of a formal argument. You will learn the rhetorical devices that make language powerful. You will analyze the greatest speeches in history and understand why they worked. And you will learn to catch yourself when your own arguments are weak — because the person who detects their own fallacies before their opponent does is the person who argues with real integrity.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Aristotle's three modes of persuasion** — ethos, pathos, logos: understanding what each is, how each works, when each is appropriate, and how each can be misused
2. **The Toulmin model of argumentation** — claim, evidence, warrant, qualifier, rebuttal: the formal structure of any complete argument
3. **Rhetorical devices** — the specific language techniques that make arguments more powerful: metaphor, anaphora, antithesis, rhetorical question, tricolon, and others
4. **Arrangement and style** — the classical canons of rhetoric: how arguments are organized (invention, arrangement) and how they are expressed (style, memory, delivery)
5. **Speech analysis** — the ability to take apart a published speech and identify exactly how it works
6. **Logical fallacies — the offensive dimension** — detecting and eliminating fallacies in your own arguments
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Aristotle's Three Appeals

Aristotle identified three modes of persuasion in his treatise *Rhetoric*, written around 350 BCE. They have not been improved upon since.

**Ethos — The Appeal to Character and Credibility**

Ethos is the reason you listen to some people and not others. Before you evaluate a person's argument, you evaluate the person. Are they credible? Do they know what they are talking about? Are they honest? Do they have your interests at heart?

A doctor who tells you to take a medicine is more persuasive than a stranger on the street who gives the same advice — because the doctor has ethos. A person who admits their argument has limitations is more persuasive than a person who claims their argument is perfect — because intellectual honesty builds ethos.

**How ethos works in practice:**
- **Expertise:** Demonstrating knowledge of the subject. Citing sources. Showing you have done the work.
- **Character:** Being known as honest. Admitting when you are wrong. Not exaggerating evidence.
- **Goodwill:** Showing that you care about the audience, not just about winning. "I am making this argument because it affects all of us" builds ethos. "I am making this argument because I want to win" destroys it.

**How ethos is destroyed:** A single act of dishonesty. Once an audience catches a speaker lying, misrepresenting evidence, or arguing in bad faith, ethos is gone — and it is almost impossible to rebuild. This is why IC1's character formation matters: the speaker whose ethos is genuine, built on a foundation of real integrity, has an advantage that no technique can replicate.

**Pathos — The Appeal to Emotion**

Pathos is the emotional dimension of an argument — the part that makes the audience feel the stakes, care about the outcome, and connect personally to what is being argued.

Pathos is not manipulation. It is the honest acknowledgment that human beings are emotional creatures and that arguments which engage only the intellect fail to move people to action. The cancer researcher who presents data about survival rates is using logos. The cancer researcher who tells the story of one patient — her name, her family, her fight — is using pathos. Neither is dishonest. Together, they are far more powerful than either alone.

**How pathos works in practice:**
- **Narrative:** Telling stories that embody the argument. A policy argument about homelessness becomes real when you describe one person's experience.
- **Vivid language:** "Children are going to bed hungry" is stronger than "food insecurity affects minors."
- **Emotional identification:** Helping the audience see themselves in the situation. "Imagine if this happened to your family."

**When pathos becomes manipulation:** When emotion is used to distract from weak evidence. When a speaker makes you feel so strongly that you do not notice the logic is missing. When a story is fabricated or distorted to produce a response the facts do not support. The line between honest emotional appeal and emotional manipulation is one of the most important lines in all of rhetoric.

**Logos — The Appeal to Logic and Evidence**

Logos is the logical structure of the argument itself. Is the reasoning valid? Does the evidence support the claim? Are the premises true? Do the conclusions follow from the premises?

Logos is what makes an argument survive examination. Ethos makes you listen. Pathos makes you care. Logos makes the argument actually hold together when someone pushes back on it.

**How logos works in practice:**
- **Evidence:** Facts, data, statistics, expert testimony, documented examples that support the claim
- **Logical structure:** Premises that lead to a conclusion through valid reasoning. "All citizens should have a voice in government [premise]. Children are future citizens [premise]. Therefore, education should prepare children to participate in government [conclusion]."
- **Addressing counterarguments:** Acknowledging the strongest case against your position and explaining why your position is still stronger

**When logos fails:** When the evidence is cherry-picked (selecting only evidence that supports your case while ignoring evidence that contradicts it). When the logical structure contains a hidden error. When statistics are technically accurate but misleading. Logos without honesty is sophistry — the appearance of logic without its substance.

### Part 2: The Toulmin Model — The Architecture of an Argument

Stephen Toulmin, a twentieth-century philosopher, developed a model of argument structure that is practical and powerful. Here is the model:

**Claim:** What you are arguing. "Schools should teach financial literacy."

**Evidence (Grounds):** The facts that support your claim. "Studies show that 44% of American adults cannot cover an unexpected $400 expense. Financial illiteracy is linked to higher debt, lower savings, and worse retirement outcomes."

**Warrant:** The reasoning that connects the evidence to the claim — the "because" that makes the evidence relevant. "Because the purpose of education is to prepare students for adult life, and adult life requires financial competence, schools should address this gap."

**Qualifier:** The conditions or limitations of your claim. "This applies primarily to public education systems that have control over curriculum design." The qualifier shows intellectual honesty — you are not claiming more than your evidence supports.

**Rebuttal:** The strongest counterargument and your response to it. "Some argue that financial literacy is the parents' responsibility. However, the evidence shows that many parents lack this knowledge themselves, creating a cycle that only institutional education can break."

A complete argument has all five elements. An argument missing its warrant is disconnected — the evidence exists but the audience does not see why it matters. An argument missing its rebuttal is fragile — it falls apart the moment someone raises the obvious objection. An argument missing its qualifier is overreaching — it claims certainty where none exists.

### Part 3: Rhetorical Devices — The Tools of Powerful Language

Rhetorical devices are specific techniques of language that make arguments more memorable, more rhythmic, and more powerful. They are not decoration. They are engineering — the structural choices that determine whether a sentence hits or misses.

**Anaphora** — repeating the same word or phrase at the beginning of successive sentences or clauses. "We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields and in the streets." (Churchill) The repetition builds rhythm and momentum.

**Antithesis** — placing contrasting ideas in parallel structure. "Ask not what your country can do for you — ask what you can do for your country." (Kennedy) The contrast sharpens both ideas.

**Tricolon** — a series of three parallel elements. "Government of the people, by the people, for the people." (Lincoln) Three is the natural rhythm of emphasis — two feels incomplete, four starts to drag.

**Rhetorical question** — a question asked for effect, not for an answer. "Are we to stand by while our children suffer?" The audience already knows the answer. The question forces them to feel it.

**Metaphor** — describing one thing in terms of another. "The iron curtain has descended across the continent." (Churchill) The metaphor makes the abstract (political division) concrete and vivid (a physical barrier).

**Chiasmus** — reversing the order of words in parallel phrases. "When the going gets tough, the tough get going." The reversal creates a satisfying click of meaning.

### Part 4: Speech Analysis — Taking Apart the Machine

When you analyze a speech, you are reverse-engineering it — figuring out how it works by examining its parts.

**The analysis protocol:**
1. **Identify the thesis.** What is the speaker arguing? State it in one sentence.
2. **Map the structure.** What is the opening? What are the main points? How does it conclude?
3. **Identify the appeals.** Where does the speaker use ethos? Pathos? Logos? Which dominates?
4. **Find the devices.** What rhetorical devices are deployed? Where? What effect do they create?
5. **Assess the evidence.** Is the evidence strong, weak, or missing? Is it honestly presented?
6. **Evaluate effectiveness.** Does the speech achieve its purpose? Why or why not?

**Practice:** Apply this protocol to King's "I Have a Dream." The thesis: the promise of American equality has not been fulfilled, and the time to fulfill it is now. The structure: historical grounding, current injustice, vision of the future, call to action. The dominant appeals: pathos (the dream imagery, the emotional identification with suffering) and ethos (King's moral authority). The devices: anaphora ("I have a dream that..."), metaphor ("the promissory note"), antithesis ("not by the color of their skin but by the content of their character"). The evidence: the Constitution, the Declaration of Independence, the lived reality of segregation. Effectiveness: one of the most consequential speeches in American history.

### Part 5: Logical Fallacies — Catching Yourself

1.7 teaches you to detect fallacies aimed at you. This packet teaches you to catch them in your own reasoning — which is harder, because you want your own arguments to be right.

**Straw man:** Misrepresenting the opponent's position to make it easier to attack. You say they argued X when they actually argued Y. Check: "Am I attacking what they actually said, or a weaker version of it?"

**Ad hominem:** Attacking the person instead of the argument. "You can't trust his opinion on education because he dropped out of college." Check: "Am I responding to their argument or to their biography?"

**Appeal to authority:** Treating an authority's opinion as proof. "Dr. Smith says it's true, so it must be true." Check: "Is this person an authority on this specific topic? Is their opinion backed by evidence?"

**False dilemma:** Presenting only two options when more exist. "Either we ban all cars or we accept pollution." Check: "Are these really the only two options?"

**Slippery slope:** Asserting without evidence that one action will lead to a catastrophic chain of events. "If we allow this, then X will happen, then Y, then everything collapses." Check: "Is there evidence for each step in this chain?"

**Circular reasoning:** Using the conclusion as a premise. "This book is great because it is a classic, and it is a classic because it is great." Check: "Am I assuming what I am trying to prove?"

The hardest fallacy to detect is the one you are committing. Build the habit of reviewing your own arguments with the same rigor you apply to others'.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Ethos, Pathos, Logos Identification [Ongoing]
The student reads or listens to a speech, advertisement, editorial, or argument and identifies which appeals are being used, where, and to what effect. At least once per week. Start with obvious examples (political speeches, commercials) and progress to subtler ones (conversations, news articles).

### Study 2: Toulmin Model Practice [Monthly]
The student constructs one complete argument per month using the full Toulmin model: claim, evidence, warrant, qualifier, rebuttal. The argument can be written or oral. The mentor evaluates each component for strength and honesty.

### Study 3: Speech Analysis [Monthly]
The student analyzes one great speech per month using the analysis protocol. Written or oral analysis covering thesis, structure, appeals, devices, evidence, and effectiveness.

### Study 4: Rhetorical Device Hunt [Bi-Weekly]
The student identifies rhetorical devices in published speeches, in advertising, in their own writing, and in everyday conversation. They maintain a running collection of examples, organized by device.

### Study 5: Fallacy Self-Check [Ongoing]
After constructing any argument — in writing, in speech, or in debate — the student reviews it for logical fallacies. The mentor also reviews and flags any the student missed. The goal: catching your own fallacies before someone else does.

### Study 6: The Persuasion-Manipulation Line [Mid-Packet Discussion]
A structured discussion: When does persuasion become manipulation? Use specific examples. The test: "If the person I am persuading knew everything I know, would they still agree? Or does my argument depend on them not knowing something?" If the latter, it is manipulation.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Three Appeals Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can identify ethos, pathos, and logos in published speeches and in everyday arguments. They understand how each works and when each is appropriate. They can explain how the same appeal can be used honestly or dishonestly.

**Developing slowly:** The student confuses the appeals or can only identify the most obvious examples (an emotional story = pathos). Cannot identify ethos or logos in practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student analyzing enough real examples? One speech per month is the minimum. Are you discussing the appeals in everyday contexts — conversations, advertisements, news stories?

### 2. Toulmin Model Competence
**Developing well:** The student can construct complete arguments with all five elements. The warrant is explicit and the rebuttal addresses the real strongest counterargument.

**Developing slowly:** Arguments are missing warrants (the evidence and claim are stated but the connection is not). Rebuttals address weak counterarguments rather than the strongest one.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you explicitly taught the warrant as a separate skill? The warrant is the hardest element because most people leave it implied. Require the student to state it.

### 3. Rhetorical Device Recognition
**Developing well:** The student identifies multiple devices in published speeches and can explain the effect each creates. They begin using devices deliberately in their own writing and speaking.

**Developing slowly:** The student can name devices from a list but cannot find them in real text. They do not use them in their own work.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student doing the "device hunt" regularly? Are they reading speeches aloud to hear the rhythm and effect of the devices?

### 4. Self-Fallacy Detection
**Developing well:** The student reviews their own arguments and catches at least some fallacies before someone else does. They accept correction when fallacies are pointed out.

**Developing slowly:** The student identifies fallacies in others' arguments but not their own. They become defensive when fallacies in their reasoning are identified.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling self-correction? When you catch a fallacy in your own reasoning, say so aloud: "Wait — I just made a false dilemma there. Let me reconsider." This normalizes the practice.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Teaching Rhetoric as a List
Rhetorical devices, logical fallacies, and the three appeals can be reduced to flashcards and memorized. That is not rhetoric. Rhetoric is the ability to construct and deliver a persuasive argument to a real audience. If the student can define anaphora but cannot use it effectively in a speech, they have not learned rhetoric — they have learned vocabulary.

### Allowing Dishonest Arguments to Stand
When the student constructs an argument that relies on cherry-picked evidence, emotional manipulation, or a straw-manned rebuttal, call it out — even if the argument is rhetorically effective. Especially if it is rhetorically effective. The student must learn that an argument can work AND be dishonest, and that effectiveness does not equal legitimacy.

### Analyzing Without Constructing
Analysis is safer than construction. The student who only analyzes others' arguments never risks having their own arguments examined. Push for regular construction practice — one complete Toulmin argument per month at minimum, delivered to an audience and subjected to questioning.
